all_items: ['A new, firm lump, or thickened tissue in the breast or armpit', 'Changes in the size, shape, or contour of the breast', 'Skin changes such as dimpling, puckering, redness, or a texture like an orange peel', 'Nipple changes (e.g., a nipple that suddenly turns inward) or unusual discharge', 'Persistent, unusual pain in the breast or nipple Mayo Clinic +1', 'Diagnosis: If a change is detected, doctors typically use a combination of mammograms, ultrasounds, and biopsies (taking a small tissue sample) for an accurate diagnosis. Cancer Council Australia', 'Staging: Staging describes the size of the tumor and whether it has spread. Cancers are typically categorized as early-stage (contained in the breast), locally advanced, or metastatic (spread to other parts of the body). World Health Organization (WHO) +1', 'Surgery: Procedures range from removing the tumor (lumpectomy) to removing the entire breast (mastectomy).', 'Radiation Therapy: High-energy rays used to destroy remaining cancer cells.', 'Systemic Therapies: Includes chemotherapy, hormone therapy, targeted therapy, and immunotherapy. Groundbreaking advancements in genomic testing are also increasingly used to spare patients with certain tumor types from unnecessary chemotherapy. Cancer Council Australia +1', 'In Australia: Reach out to the Cancer Council Australia for screening guidelines, or the Breast Cancer Network Australia (BCNA) for patient support.', 'Global/Other Regions: Consult the World Health Organization (WHO) Breast Cancer Fact Sheet for worldwide statistics and health strategies.']

all_items: ['Benign (Non-Cancerous) Tumors: These stay in one place, do not invade surrounding tissues, and are not cancer.', 'Malignant (Cancerous) Tumors: These grow beyond their boundaries, invade nearby healthy tissues, and can spread.', 'Metastasis: This is the process where cancer cells break away from the original tumor and travel through the bloodstream or lymphatic system to form new tumors in other parts of the body. National Cancer Institute (.gov) +2', 'Carcinoma: Starts in the skin or tissues that line internal organs (e.g., breast, lung, colon cancers).', 'Sarcoma: Begins in bones, cartilage, fat, or muscles.', 'Leukemia: Cancers of the blood-forming tissue, such as the bone marrow.', 'Lymphoma & Myeloma: Cancers that start in the immune system. National Cancer Institute (.gov) +1', 'Tobacco use', 'Excessive sun exposure and UV radiation', 'Lifestyle factors like poor diet, obesity, and alcohol consumption', 'Certain viral infections (e.g., HPV) Cancer Council Australia +1']

all_items: ['Unexplained Weight Loss: A sudden, unintentional loss of 5kg (10+ pounds) or more without an active effort to diet or exercise.', 'Fatigue: Extreme, persistent tiredness that does not improve with rest.', 'Unexplained Pain: A new ache or pain anywhere in your body that has no known cause, persists, or worsens over time.', 'Fevers and Night Sweats: Recurring, unexplained fevers or heavy nighttime sweating.', 'Appetite Changes: Persistent loss of appetite or a feeling of fullness even when not eating much. Cancer Council Australia +4', 'Lumps or Swellings: A new lump, bump, or area of thickening under the skin that can be felt, particularly in the breast, neck, or lymph nodes. Mayo Clinic +1', "Skin Changes: A new mole or any noticeable change to an existing mole (e.g., in size, shape, or color). Also watch for sores that won't heal, or skin that suddenly becomes yellowed (jaundice), darkened, or red. Mayo Clinic +1", 'Changes in Bowel or Bladder Habits: Persistent constipation, diarrhea, blood in your stool/urine, or a sudden, lasting change in how frequently you need to urinate. Mayo Clinic +1', 'Abnormal Bleeding or Bruising: Spontaneous bleeding (e.g., coughing up blood, unusual vaginal bleeding, or blood in your vomit) or bruising for no known reason. Mayo Clinic +2', "Persistent Cough or Hoarseness: A cough that doesn't go away, trouble breathing, or lasting changes to your voice. Mayo Clinic +2", 'Difficulty Swallowing: Persistent indigestion, discomfort after eating, or trouble swallowing food. Mayo Clinic +1']

all_items: ['Appearance: Usually presents as a pink or pearly bump, a shiny pale area, or a glossy black/brown bump in darker skin tones.', 'Behavior: The most common type of skin cancer. It grows slowly and rarely spreads, but can invade surrounding tissue if left untreated. Cancer Council Victoria +3', 'Appearance: Often appears as a thick, rough, or scaly patch, or as a rapidly growing firm red lump. It may also look like an open sore.', 'Behavior: The second most common type. It grows faster than BCC and can spread to other parts of the body if not treated. Cancer Council Victoria +2', 'Appearance: The most dangerous type of skin cancer. It typically looks like a new, unusual spot or an existing mole that has changed.', 'Behavior: Arises from the pigment-producing cells (melanocytes). It is far less common than BCC or SCC but is much more serious because it can spread rapidly to vital organs. Mayo Clinic +5', 'A - Asymmetry: One half of the mole or spot does not match the other half.', 'B - Border: The edges are irregular, ragged, notched, or blurred.', 'C - Color: The color is uneven and may include different shades of brown or black, or patches of pink, red, white, or blue.', 'D - Diameter: The spot is larger than 6mm (about the size of a pencil eraser), although melanomas can sometimes be smaller.', 'E - Evolving: The mole is changing in size, shape, color, or elevation. It may also start to itch, crust, or bleed.', 'Use Sunscreen: Apply a broad-spectrum SPF 30+ sunscreen daily on exposed skin.', 'Seek Shade: Avoid the sun during peak UV hours (typically mid-morning to mid-afternoon).', 'Wear Protective Clothing: Wear UV-blocking sunglasses, wide-brimmed hats, and sun-protective clothing.', 'Avoid Tanning Beds: Do not use solariums or tanning lamps. The Skin Cancer Foundation +1']

all_items: ['Screening: Regular screenings are the best defense. Guidelines recommend starting routine screening (via colonoscopy or stool tests) at age 45 for individuals at average risk.', 'Survival Rate: 5-year survival is approximately 91% for localized early-stage cases, dropping to 13% if the cancer metastasizes.', 'Local Context (Australia): Australians aged 45 to 74 can access free bowel screening kits. Cancer Council Australia +4', 'Blood in your stool (bright red or dark)', 'Unexplained, persistent changes in bowel habits (diarrhoea, constipation, or a feeling of incomplete emptying)', 'Abdominal pain, bloating, or cramping', 'Unexplained weight loss or fatigue National Cancer Institute (.gov) +1', 'Age: Risk increases significantly after age 50, though rates in adults under 50 are rising.', 'Family History: Having a close relative who has had bowel cancer or polyps.', 'Lifestyle: A diet high in red or processed meats, low fiber, lack of exercise, smoking, and heavy alcohol consumption.', "Medical History: Having inflammatory bowel disease (like Crohn's) or type 2 diabetes. Mayo Clinic +1"]

all_items: ['Surgery: Often the primary treatment to physically remove a tumor or cancerous tissue.', 'Radiation Therapy: Uses high-energy rays to destroy cancer cells and shrink tumors.', 'Chemotherapy: Uses specific anti-cancer drugs to kill cancer cells throughout the body.', "Immunotherapy: Treatments that stimulate or assist the body's natural immune system to fight the cancer.", 'Targeted Therapy: Medications designed to block the growth and spread of cancer by interfering with specific molecules required for tumor growth.', 'Hormone Therapy: Uses medicines or procedures to block or remove hormones that fuel the growth of certain cancers. Cancer Council Australia +2', 'Palliative Care: Specialized care focused on relieving pain and other symptoms to improve the quality of life for patients and their families.', 'Clinical Trials: Research studies that give patients access to cutting-edge experimental treatments and medications. Cancer Council Australia +2']

all_items: ['Leukemia: Cancer of the white blood cells that usually begins in the bone marrow. It is often categorized by how quickly it progresses (acute or chronic) and the type of white blood cell affected.', 'Lymphoma: Cancer that affects the lymphocytes (immune cells). It typically causes swollen lymph nodes and can spread to the spleen, liver, and lungs. The two main types are Hodgkin and Non-Hodgkin lymphoma. Cancer Council NSW +2', 'Myeloma: A cancer of plasma cells (white blood cells that produce antibodies). It accumulates in the bone marrow, damaging bones and weakening the immune system. Cancer Council Australia +1', 'Smoking tobacco (which roughly doubles the risk).', 'Being overweight or obese.', 'A history of chronic pancreatitis or a family history of pancreatic, ovarian, or colon cancers.', 'Advancing age, with most cases diagnosed in adults over 60. Cancer Council Australia +1', 'Imaging tests such as CT scans, MRIs, or PET scans.', 'Endoscopic ultrasounds or tissue biopsies to confirm the presence of cancer.', 'Blood tests to check liver and kidney function. Cancer Council Australia', 'Surgery: Surgery is the primary option for potentially curing the cancer, but it is typically only viable for early-stage cases (about 20% of diagnoses). all_items: ['Energy Conversion: When you eat, your body breaks down carbohydrates into glucose, which enters your bloodstream. Diabetes Australia +1', 'The Role of Insulin: The pancreas produces insulin, which acts as a "key" to open cell doors, allowing glucose to enter and fuel your body. Diabetes Australia +1', 'The Problem: In diabetes, this system fails. Without enough effective insulin, glucose remains in the blood, leading to high blood sugar.', 'Type 1 Diabetes: An autoimmune disease where the body’s immune system attacks and destroys the cells in the pancreas that make insulin. People with Type 1 produce no insulin and require daily insulin to survive. National Institutes of Health (.gov) +2', "Type 2 Diabetes: The most common form. The body either doesn't produce enough insulin or resists it, meaning it cannot use the insulin properly. It is often managed with lifestyle changes, oral medication, or insulin injections. National Institutes of Health (.gov) +3", 'Gestational Diabetes: A temporary type of diabetes that develops in some women during pregnancy. It usually resolves after the baby is born but increases the risk of developing Type 2 later in life. National Institutes of Health (.gov)', 'Excessive thirst (polydipsia)', 'Frequent urination (polyuria)', 'Extreme fatigue or lack of energy', 'Unexplained weight loss', 'Blurred vision', "Slow-healing sores or frequent infections YouTube·Children's Mercy"]

all_items: ['The mechanism: Normally, the pancreas produces insulin, which acts like a key to let glucose (sugar) into your cells for energy. In Type 1, the immune system mistakenly views these insulin-producing cells as foreign and destroys them. Diabetes Victoria +1', 'The cause: It is not caused by diet, lifestyle, or weight. It is triggered by a combination of genetics and environmental factors, such as a viral infection. Baker Heart and Diabetes Institute', 'Excessive thirst and a dry mouth', 'Frequent urination (having to pee often)', 'Unexplained and rapid weight loss', 'Extreme fatigue, weakness, or mood swings', 'Blurry vision Healthdirect +1', 'Diagnosis: Doctors diagnose it using blood tests that measure your blood glucose levels and autoantibodies. Mayo Clinic', 'Insulin Therapy: People living with Type 1 diabetes must take insulin every day. This is delivered via multiple daily injections or an insulin pump. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention | CDC (.gov) +1', "Monitoring: Blood glucose levels are tracked constantly using finger-prick tests or continuous glucose monitors (CGMs). Murdoch Children's Research Institute (MCRI)", 'Emergency Risk: If left untreated, it can lead to diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), a life-threatening medical emergency where the body breaks down fat too quickly and produces toxic acids. all_items: ['The mechanism: Normally, the pancreas produces insulin, which acts like a key to let glucose (sugar) into your cells for energy. In Type 1, the immune system mistakenly views these insulin-producing cells as foreign and destroys them. Diabetes Victoria +1', 'The cause: It is not caused by diet, lifestyle, or weight. It is triggered by a combination of genetics and environmental factors, such as a viral infection. Baker Heart and Diabetes Institute', 'Excessive thirst and a dry mouth', 'Frequent urination (having to pee often)', 'Unexplained and rapid weight loss', 'Extreme fatigue, weakness, or mood swings', 'Blurry vision Healthdirect +1', 'Diagnosis: Doctors diagnose it using blood tests that measure your blood glucose levels and autoantibodies. Mayo Clinic', 'Insulin Therapy: People living with Type 1 diabetes must take insulin every day. This is delivered via multiple daily injections or an insulin pump. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention | CDC (.gov) +1', "Monitoring: Blood glucose levels are tracked constantly using finger-prick tests or continuous glucose monitors (CGMs). Murdoch Children's Research Institute (MCRI)", 'Emergency Risk: If left untreated, it can lead to diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), a life-threatening medical emergency where the body breaks down fat too quickly and produces toxic acids. Mayo Clinic']

all_items: ['Glucose Control: When you eat, your body breaks carbohydrates down into glucose. Insulin acts as a "key" that unlocks cell membranes, allowing glucose to enter the cells for fuel. NDSS', 'Storage: Insulin also signals the liver to store excess glucose for later use. Diabetes Australia', 'Energy Balance: Without sufficient insulin, your cells become energy-deprived while blood sugar levels dangerously rise (hyperglycemia).', 'Rapid-Acting: Starts working in about 15 minutes; used around meal times.', 'Short-Acting: Starts working in 30 to 60 minutes.', 'Intermediate-Acting: Takes effect in 1 to 3 hours and lasts 12 to 18 hours.', 'Long/Ultra-Long-Acting: Provides a steady, background "basal" dose that can last 24 hours or more. Diabetes Australia', 'Syringes or Pens', 'Insulin Pumps (wearable devices that provide a continuous flow)']

all_items: ['HbA1c Test: Measures your average blood glucose over the past 2 to 3 months. A result of $6.5\\%$ ( $48 \\text{ mmol/mol}$) or higher generally indicates diabetes.', 'Fasting Blood Glucose Test: Taken after you have not eaten for at least 8 hours. A result of $7.0 \\text{ mmol/L}$ or higher suggests diabetes.', "Oral Glucose Tolerance Test (OGTT): Measures your body's ability to process a sugary drink. Fasting blood is drawn, you consume a glucose drink, and your blood is tested again after 1 or 2 hours.", 'Self-Assessment: You can estimate your risk of developing type 2 diabetes over the next five years by using the Australian Type 2 Diabetes Risk Assessment Tool (AUSDRISK).', 'Professional Screening: It is recommended that non-Indigenous individuals get their risk checked every 3 years starting at age 40, and annually for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples aged 18 and older. Healthdirect']

How can you prevent or control hypertension? ❤️ The best thing to do to prevent or control hypertension is regular exercise. It is recommended to aim for at least 30 minutes of moderate physical activity each day. all_items: ['Diet: Adopt a heart-healthy diet rich in whole grains, fruits, and vegetables, such as the DASH (Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension) diet. Healthdirect +1', 'Sodium Reduction: $2,300\\text{ mg}$', 'Exercise: Engage in 30 to 60 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic physical activity (e.g., brisk walking, cycling) on most days of the week. Healthdirect +1', 'Weight Management: Maintain a healthy weight; even a modest weight loss can noticeably drop blood pressure. Queensland Health +1', 'Substance Reduction: Limit alcohol consumption and strictly avoid smoking or vaping. Healthdirect +1', 'ACE Inhibitors: Relax blood vessels to reduce blood pressure and protect the kidneys (e.g., lisinopril, enalapril).', 'Angiotensin II Receptor Blockers (ARBs): Work similarly to ACE inhibitors by relaxing blood vessels (e.g., losartan, telmisartan).', 'Calcium Channel Blockers (CCBs): Prevent calcium from entering the heart and blood vessel walls, allowing blood vessels to widen (e.g., amlodipine, felodipine).', 'Diuretics ("Water Pills"): Help the kidneys remove excess sodium and water from the body (e.g., hydrochlorothiazide, chlorthalidone). Mayo Clinic +1']

all_items: ['Severe headaches: Often throbbing, worse in the mornings, and exacerbated by lying down, coughing, or sneezing.', 'Vision changes: Blurry vision, double vision (diplopia), or temporary episodes of vision dimming.', 'Papilledema: Swelling of the optic nerve, which requires monitoring to prevent permanent vision loss.', 'Pulsatile tinnitus: A rhythmic "whooshing" or buzzing sound in the ears that syncs with your heartbeat.', 'Nausea, dizziness, or neck stiffness. Cleveland Clinic +5', 'Idiopathic (IIH or Pseudotumor Cerebri): High pressure without an obvious cause, most common in women of childbearing age.', 'Secondary Intracranial Hypertension: Caused by underlying medical issues or triggers, such as head injuries, certain medications (e.g., tetracycline antibiotics, steroids, vitamin A derivatives), venous sinus thrombosis (blood clots in the brain), or sleep apnea. Cleveland Clinic +3', 'Eye and vision exams: To check for papilledema or blind spots.', 'Neuroimaging: An MRI or CT scan of the brain.', 'Lumbar puncture (spinal tap): To measure the exact CSF pressure. Cleveland Clinic +1', 'Weight management: For IIH, even modest weight loss can lead to remission.', 'Medications: Prescription drugs like acetazolamide (Diamox) are frequently used to reduce CSF production.', 'Surgical Options: If vision loss progresses or medication is ineffective, procedures like an optic nerve sheath fenestration (to relieve pressure on the optic nerve), a CSF shunt (to drain excess fluid), or transverse sinus stenting may be performed. Optic Nerve Canberra +1']

all_items: ['Emotional: Persistent feelings of sadness, emptiness, hopelessness, tearfulness, or unexplained irritability.', 'Physical: Extreme tiredness, lack of energy, disrupted sleep (insomnia or oversleeping), and changes in appetite.', 'Cognitive: Difficulty concentrating, making decisions, or remembering things.', 'Behavioral: Withdrawing from friends and family, and a loss of interest or pleasure in once-enjoyed hobbies.', 'Serious: Recurrent thoughts of death or suicide. Mayo Clinic +3', 'Brain Chemistry: Imbalances in naturally occurring brain chemicals (neurotransmitters) can affect mood.', 'Genetics: Having blood relatives who suffer from depression can increase your risk.', 'Life Events: Trauma, severe losses, chronic stress, or major life changes (like losing a job) can trigger episodes.', 'Physical Health: Chronic illnesses or hormonal changes (such as postpartum or menopause) can play a significant role. Mayo Clinic +2', 'Psychological Therapies: Talking with a mental health professional (e.g., counseling, Cognitive Behavioral Therapy).', 'Medication: Antidepressant medications prescribed by a doctor.', 'Lifestyle Changes: Regular exercise, a healthy diet, and maintaining a routine. World Health Organization (WHO) +3']

Mayo Clinic Health System +3', 'Psychotherapy: Talk therapy (such as Cognitive Behavioral Therapy or CBT) is a highly effective treatment for both.', 'Medication: Antidepressants are often prescribed to regulate brain chemistry.', 'Lifestyle Changes: Regular exercise, healthy sleep habits, and social support can significantly reduce symptoms. all_items: ['Uterine Involution: Your uterus gradually shrinks from the size of a watermelon to a pear, often accompanied by cramping.', 'Lochia: You will experience vaginal bleeding that is initially heavy and tapers off over several weeks.', 'Healing: Vaginal tears, grazes, or Cesarean section incisions require careful hygiene and rest to heal properly.', 'Baby Blues: Mild teariness, anxiety, or mood swings in the first two weeks are common and typically resolve on their own.', "Postpartum Depression (PPD): If feelings of sadness, hopelessness, or an inability to bond with your baby last longer than two weeks, you may have PPD. PPD is a treatable medical condition and requires professional help. Office on Women's Health (.gov) +2", 'Rest: Limit physical exertion and prioritize sleep whenever the newborn sleeps.', 'Nutrition: Eat a balanced diet and stay hydrated, especially if you are breastfeeding.', 'Ask for Help: Accept assistance from family and friends with cooking, cleaning, and running errands.', 'Medical Follow-ups: Attend your routine six-week postpartum check-up to ensure your body is healing appropriately. Cleveland Clinic +4']

World Health Organization (WHO) +1', 'Europe and Asia: Causes Hemorrhagic Fever with Renal Syndrome (HFRS), which primarily attacks the kidneys and blood vessels, with fatality rates ranging from <1% to 15%. World Health Organization (WHO) +1', 'Australia: There are no recorded human cases of hantavirus, though local rodents carry related arenaviruses.', 'Rodent Exposure: You become infected by breathing in aerosolized dust contaminated with infected rodent urine, feces, or saliva, or by direct contact.', 'Incubation Period: Symptoms generally appear 1 to 8 weeks after exposure.', 'Early Symptoms: Resemble a severe flu—fatigue, fever, general muscle aches, and headaches.', 'Late Symptoms: Progresses rapidly into coughing, shortness of breath, and respiratory failure, or severe kidney failure. Australian Centre for Disease Control +4', 'Treatment: There is no specific vaccine or antiviral cure. Immediate, supportive care in an Intensive Care Unit (ICU) is the standard treatment. Australian Centre for Disease Control +1', 'Prevention: The best way to avoid hantavirus is by safely cleaning rodent-infested areas. When sweeping or cleaning droppings, avoid aerosolizing the dust—wear gloves, use a mask, and wet the area with disinfectant. YouTube·UC Davis Health']

World Health Organization (WHO) +1', 'Human-to-Human Transmission: Almost all strains can only be caught from animals. The Andes virus (found in South America) is the only known exception, where very rare, limited human-to-human transmission has been documented through close, prolonged contact.', 'Regional Variations: The World Health Organization (WHO) and the Centers for Disease Control (CDC) track the virus globally, noting that the risk to the general public remains low. Australian Broadcasting Corporation +2', 'Hantavirus Cardiopulmonary Syndrome (HCPS / HPS): Prevalent in North and South America. It begins with flu-like symptoms (fever, fatigue, muscle aches) and rapidly progresses to coughing, shortness of breath, and fluid filling the lungs, which can be fatal in up to 50% of cases. Australian Broadcasting Corporation +1', 'Hemorrhagic Fever with Renal Syndrome (HFRS): Prevalent in Europe and Asia. Symptoms include severe back and abdominal pain, low blood pressure, bleeding disorders, and kidney failure. World Health Organization (WHO) +1']

all_items: ['Acute Illness: Symptoms usually begin 2 to 14 days after exposure and the active phase typically resolves within days to a couple of weeks. A lingering cough or fatigue may persist slightly longer. Johns Hopkins Medicine +1', 'Severe Cases: For those with severe disease, the active illness can last for several months and may require hospitalization. Healthdirect +1', 'Long COVID: If symptoms continue for more than 12 weeks after the initial infection, it may be diagnosed as Long COVID. These lingering effects—such as brain fog, fatigue, or altered senses—can last for months or, in some cases, a year or longer.']

all_items: ['Symptom-Specific Medications: Doctors often prescribe drugs to target particular issues. Examples include low-dose naltrexone for pain and fatigue, antihistamines for inflammation, or medications for rapid heart rate/POTS. National Institutes of Health (.gov) +1', 'Energy Pacing and Rest: If you experience post-exertional malaise (where symptoms worsen after mental or physical exertion), resting and "pacing" your daily activities is widely recommended. Healthdirect +1', 'Rehabilitation: Pulmonary rehabilitation, breathing exercises, and physical or mental health rehabilitation programs are commonly utilized to help rebuild strength.', 'Olfactory Training: For those with lingering loss of smell and taste, nerve retraining exercises may help regain sensory function. Mayo Clinic +1', 'Supplements: Coenzyme Q10 and other dietary supplements are frequently used by patients to manage lingering fatigue and brain fog. National Institutes of Health (.gov) +1']

all_items: ["Eastern Perspective: In TCM, health depends on the body's vital energy, called qi (chi), flowing smoothly through channels called meridians. Illness or pain occurs when qi is blocked. Acupuncturists insert needles to unblock these pathways and restore balance. Johns Hopkins Medicine +1", "Western Perspective: Modern science suggests that the needles stimulate the central nervous system. This triggers the release of endorphins (the body's natural painkillers) and other neurohormones that can relieve pain and alter the way the body experiences it.", 'Consultation: A licensed acupuncturist will evaluate your health history and symptoms.', 'Needle Insertion: Between 3 to 15 hair-thin, single-use steel needles are placed into specific points on the body.', 'Stimulation: The needles are left in place for 20 to 30 minutes and may be gently manipulated by hand or stimulated with small electric currents (electroacupuncture). GoodRx +2', 'Pain Management: Back, knee, and neck pain, as well as osteoarthritis and tension headaches.', 'Wellness & Relief: Stress and anxiety, insomnia, allergy symptoms, and digestive issues. Mayo Clinic +2']

Commonly used for headaches, dental pain, and tension.', 'Stomach 36 (ST 36 / Zusanli): Located on the lower leg, about 3 cun below the knee cap. Often stimulated to boost energy, improve digestion, and reduce inflammation.', 'Pericardium 6 (PC 6 / Neiguan): Located on the inner wrist, between the two central tendons. Highly effective for nausea, motion sickness, and anxiety.', 'Liver 3 (LR 3 / Taichong): Located on the top of the foot in the depression between the first and second toe bones. Used for stress relief, menstrual cramps, and emotional balancing.', 'Governor Vessel 20 (GV 20 / Baihui): Located at the very top of the center of the head. Used to calm the mind, elevate mood, and treat dizziness.']

Wade decision. Wikipedia +1', 'State-by-State Patchwork: Since federal protections were eliminated, abortion legality is determined at the state level. You can use the Guttmacher Institute Abortion Policy Tracker to see current laws and gestational limits by state. YouTube·Scripps News', 'Abortion Pills (Mifepristone): Access to medication abortion remains a major legal battlefield. The Supreme Court unanimously rejected an initial challenge to restrict access to mifepristone in 2024, but lower court rulings and state-level lawsuits continually challenge the distribution of the drug by mail. BBC +3', 'State & Territory Responsibility: There is no explicit constitutional right to an abortion in Australia. Instead, each state and territory manages its own criminal, health, and medical legislation. Law Society Journal +1', 'Decriminalization: Abortion has been effectively decriminalized across all Australian jurisdictions, though gestational limits, the required number of doctors\' approvals, and out-of-pocket costs vary significantly depending on where you are (often referred to as a "postcode lottery"). Law Society Journal +1', 'High Court Rulings: In April 2019, the High Court of Australia unanimously upheld Victorian and Tasmanian laws establishing "safe access zones" (no-protest zones) around abortion clinics. The Court ruled that protecting the safety, dignity, and privacy of women accessing reproductive healthcare is a constitutionally valid objective. The Conversation +3']

all_items: ['Transmission: HIV is transmitted via specific bodily fluids (blood, semen, vaginal and rectal fluids, and breast milk). The most common ways it spreads are through unprotected sexual intercourse and sharing injecting drug equipment. Healthdirect +1', 'Diagnosis: You cannot tell if you have HIV based on symptoms alone; the only way to know is to get tested with a blood or rapid test. HealthyWA', 'Treatment: HIV is a chronic but highly treatable condition managed with Antiretroviral Therapy (ART).', 'Life Expectancy: People with HIV who take effective daily ART as prescribed can achieve an undetectable viral load. When this happens, they cannot sexually transmit the virus to others (known as U=U, or Undetectable = Untransmissible) and can live a long, healthy life with a normal life expectancy. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention | CDC (.gov) +1', 'Fever and headache', 'Rash', 'Sore throat', 'Swollen lymph nodes World Health Organization (WHO) +1', 'PrEP (Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis): A daily pill or long-acting injection taken by HIV-negative individuals to prevent HIV infection before exposure.', 'PEP (Post-Exposure Prophylaxis): Emergency medication taken within 72 hours of a potential HIV exposure to prevent infection.', 'Australia: Find local testing sites, healthcare providers, and support services through Healthdirect Australia or get peer support and information from NAPWHA (National Association of People with HIV Australia).', 'Global: For general health facts, travel advisories, and global strategies, visit the World Health Organization (WHO) HIV Portal.']

all_items: ['HIV: The virus itself. It damages the immune system but can be managed to the point where it never progresses to AIDS.', "AIDS: The syndrome (a collection of symptoms and illnesses) that occurs when the immune system is severely weakened by HIV. It is diagnosed when a person's CD4 white blood cell count falls below 200 cells per cubic millimeter of blood, or when they develop specific serious illnesses.", 'Blood', 'Semen and pre-seminal fluid', 'Rectal and vaginal fluids', 'Breast milk', 'It is not spread through casual, day-to-day contact like kissing, hugging, sharing food, or toilet seats. World Health Organization (WHO)', 'Antiretroviral Therapy (ART): This involves taking daily medications that stop the virus from multiplying, allowing the immune system to recover. Healthdirect +1', '$U=U$ People living with HIV who take their ART as prescribed can reduce the virus to undetectable levels. When the viral load is undetectable, the virus cannot be transmitted to sexual partners. NSW Health', 'Preventative Measures: Pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) and Post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) are highly effective medications used by HIV-negative individuals to prevent infection after potential exposure.']

all_items: ['Top Surgery: Reshapes the chest. For transmasculine individuals, this usually involves a bilateral mastectomy or subcutaneous mastectomy to remove breast tissue. For transfeminine individuals, it involves breast augmentation using implants.', 'Bottom Surgery: Refers to genital reconstruction. For transmasculine individuals, this can involve metoidioplasty, phalloplasty, vaginectomy, or the construction of a neophallus. For transfeminine individuals, it involves procedures like orchiectomy (removal of testicles), vulvoplasty, or vaginoplasty (creation of a vagina).', "Facial Procedures: Alters facial features to align with an individual's gender. Facial feminization surgery (FFS) includes brow lifts, chin and jaw reshaping, rhinoplasty, and Adam's apple reduction. Facial masculinization surgery may involve chin or jaw augmentation and forehead lengthening.", 'Voice & Body Contouring: Includes voice therapy or procedures like Wendler glottoplasty (vocal cord shortening) to raise pitch. Body contouring procedures, such as liposuction or fat grafting, can also be utilized. Johns Hopkins Medicine +8', 'Hormone Replacement Therapy (HRT): The use of estrogen/anti-androgens or testosterone to develop secondary sex characteristics.', 'Hair Removal: Electrolysis or laser hair removal for facial and body hair.', 'Legal & Social: Updating name and gender markers on legal documents, and social affirmation such as adopting chosen pronouns, names, and clothing styles. Wikipedia +4']
